planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in water or moist potting mix at any time indoors, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained potting mix or garden soil in partial to full shade outdoors after frost in zones 10–12; pothos is the most low-maintenance and forgiving of all tropical foliage plants, tolerating conditions that kill most other plants.

directions: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix and grow in partial to full shade; pothos (devil's ivy, golden pothos) is the most widely grown and universally recognized houseplant in the world — a vigorous tropical vine from the Solomon Islands with glossy, heart-shaped, variably yellow-and-green or solid-green marbled leaves that trails or climbs on virtually any surface, rooting at the nodes to attach to trellises, moss poles, and textured walls. Its legendary low-maintenance character — tolerating low light, irregular watering, low humidity, and neglect that kills competing tropical plants — makes it the most appropriate choice for shaded interior spaces, covered porches, and areas of deep garden shade in zones 10–12, where it covers the ground or climbs trees. Stem cuttings root within days in plain water and can be transplanted to soil or left to grow in vases. Classified as an invasive species in some tropical regions (Hawaii, Florida) because of its ability to smother native vegetation outdoors in frost-free climates. Common cultivars include 'Golden Queen' (yellow-green variegated), 'Marble Queen' (white-splashed), 'Neon' (chartreuse), and 'N'Joy' (white and green). All parts toxic if ingested.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in water or moist potting mix at any time of year, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile potting mix in bright indirect light to low light; heartleaf philodendron is the most shade-tolerant and easy-to-root of the landscape philodendrons.

directions: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix and grow in partial to full shade; heartleaf philodendron is a classic tropical vining houseplant and shade garden perennial with deep-green, glossy, distinctly heart-shaped leaves (3–4 inches at maturity, much larger when climbing in tropical conditions) on long, trailing stems that root readily at every node. It is considerably more shade-tolerant than pothos and produces a uniformly rich, velvety, dark-green appearance that remains attractive across a very wide range of light conditions — from bright indirect light down to quite dim interior corners. New leaves emerge with a bronze-copper tint before maturing to deep forest-green, a distinctive and attractive feature of the young growth. In zones 10–12 it covers the ground under trees and climbs tree trunks as a large-leaved epiphytic vine. Extremely easy to propagate — a single node cutting placed in water produces roots within days. 'Brasil' (yellow-green variegated center stripe), 'Micans' (velvety bronze-green), and 'Lemon Lime' (chartreuse) are popular cultivars. All parts toxic if ingested.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or divide basal offshoots and plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in bright indirect light to deep shade; Chinese evergreen is among the most low-light-tolerant foliage plants available — dark green cultivars survive in quite dim conditions, while variegated or colored cultivars require more light to maintain their pattern.

directions: Root stem cuttings or divide offshoots in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in bright indirect light to deep shade; Chinese evergreen is one of the most tolerant and adaptable of all tropical foliage plants, suited to interior conditions of very low light intensity that defeat most other tropical houseplants. The lance-shaped to elliptic leaves are produced in erect rosettes and may be plain dark green, or variegated in combinations of silver-gray, light-green, white, cream, pink, red, and orange depending on cultivar — modern breeding has produced cultivars in a remarkably broad color range. The plant tolerates irregular watering, low humidity, and dim fluorescent light with less decline than virtually any other ornamental plant, making it a staple of interior plantscaping in offices, shopping malls, and shaded building lobbies. It is one of several plants demonstrated in NASA clean-air studies to filter certain indoor air pollutants. 'Silver Queen' (silver-green), 'Emerald Beauty' (dark-green), 'Red Siam' (deep red-edged), and 'Super White' are widely grown selections. All parts toxic if ingested.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile potting mix or garden soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; dwarf umbrella tree adapts readily to a wide range of light conditions but develops the most compact, attractive growth with some direct or very bright indirect light.

directions: Root stem-tip cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; dwarf umbrella tree is a compact, versatile tropical shrub from Taiwan that produces the same distinctive, deeply divided, umbrella-shaped, palmate leaf compound structure as its larger relative Schefflera actinophylla but remains a more manageable size for garden and indoor use — each leaf consists of 7–9 glossy, oval leaflets radiating from a central point like the spokes of an umbrella, creating a bold, tropical texture on densely branched shrubs. In zones 10–12 it grows as a substantial landscape shrub or small tree; elsewhere it is among the most widely grown tropical houseplants, adapting to a broad range of indoor light conditions while remaining significantly more compact and manageable than the large umbrella tree. It can be pruned aggressively to maintain shape and size, trained as a braided trunk or multi-stem specimen, or allowed to grow freely. 'Trinette' (yellow-variegated), 'Gold Capella' (golden-green), and 'Janine' (cream-variegated) are popular cultivars. Toxic if ingested.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; snow bush develops the most vibrant white, pink, and red coloring in full sun but tolerates and performs acceptably in partial shade.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; snow bush (snowbush, calico plant) is a Pacific Islands tropical shrub that produces some of the most elaborately variegated foliage available in tropical landscaping — the small, oval leaves are mottled, splashed, spotted, and streaked with white, pink, red, and green in patterns that vary from leaf to leaf and intensify in color through the growing season, creating a continuously shifting kaleidoscope of color. Young growth is especially vibrant, flushing pink and red before developing its characteristic multi-colored pattern. The cascading, arching branch habit creates a graceful, cottage-garden character despite the dramatic coloring. Excellent as a colorful accent in tropical mixed borders, as a container specimen, and as a vibrant informal hedge in zones 10–12. Full sun intensifies the coloring; partial shade softens it. Frost-sensitive — protect or bring indoors when temperatures approach 35°F. The cultivar 'Roseo-Picta' is the standard ornamental selection with the fullest color range.
